Ocean Sessions Dataviz Challenge 2026 · Gulf of Guinea · Badagry, Nigeria

When the Water Remembers

Three decades of real Copernicus Marine Service data — sea surface temperature, sea level anomaly, and chlorophyll-a — visualized from the shoreline of Badagry, Nigeria.

REAL CMEMS DATA · GLORYS12 · DUACS · GlobColour L4 · 1993–2025

By Sunday Kenneth Shaho

“The sea no longer behaves the way our fathers knew it. The tides rise differently. Fish move farther away. Some seasons feel hotter. Sometimes the water looks greener than usual, sometimes strangely empty. We notice these things. We have always noticed. Now the satellites confirm what we already knew.” — A fisherman from Badagry, Gulf of Guinea coastline
ACT I · BLUE OCEANThe Gulf Has Forgotten How to Cool Down
ACT II · GREEN OCEANMy Mother's Net Comes Back Lighter
ACT III · BLUE OCEAN · SLAWater In The Living Room
Act I · Sea Surface Temperature · GLORYS12 cmems_mod_glo_phy_my_0.083deg_P1M-m
The Gulf Has Forgotten
How to Cool Down
thetao · Monthly mean · 1993–2025 · Gulf of Guinea region
CMEMS · MyOcean Viewer Screenshot THETAO · March 2024 Sea Surface Temperature · Gulf of Guinea
March 2024 recorded the highest single monthly SST in the 33-year dataset at 28.70°C — more than 3°C above the 1993–2016 climatological mean of 25.39°C. This MyOcean Viewer screenshot shows the actual surface temperature field across the Gulf of Guinea at the peak of the most anomalous month on record.
Source: CMEMS MyOcean Viewer · GLOBAL_MULTIYEAR_PHY_001_030 · variable: thetao · March 2024

Warming since 1993

2024

statistically significant
Monthly SST Anomaly · Gulf of Guinea · Jan 1993 – Dec 2025
Each column is one month. Color encodes SST anomaly relative to the 1993–2016 climatological mean (25.39°C). The wave-like shape mimics the ocean's thermal pulse — boreal summer upwelling cools the GoG (blues), boreal winter warms it (reds). The baseline has shifted upward: red is more common now than in the 1990s.
Cooler anomaly
Warmer anomaly
Source: CMEMS GLOBAL_MULTIYEAR_PHY_001_030 · cmems_mod_glo_phy_my_0.083deg_P1M-m · variable: thetao · anomaly = observed minus 1993–2016 mean (25.39°C)
Annual Mean SST · Raw Temperature °C · 1993–2025
Annual means of monthly sea surface temperature. The upward trend is 0.22°C per decade — statistically significant (p<0.001). The five warmest years are all after 2016: 2024, 2023, 2021, 2020, 2016.
Annual mean SST (°C)
Linear trend (+0.22°C/decade)
Top-5 warmest years
Source: same as above · annual means aggregated from monthly thetao values
"Growing up in Badagry, the sea was always warm. But the warmth we are seeing now is different — it lingers, it deepens, it does not retreat the way it once did. Fishermen who have worked these waters for forty years say the ocean feels sick. The data says the same thing."
"The baseline has shifted. This is not a bad year — it is a new normal. The coolest year since 2016 is still warmer than most years before 2010."
Act II · Chlorophyll-a · OCEANCOLOUR_GLO_BGC_L4_MY_009_104
My Mother's Net
Comes Back Lighter
chl · Monthly mean · 1993–2025 · Gulf of Guinea region · mg m⁻³

July 2014 · mg m⁻³
Aug
Peak bloom month
mean 0.321 mg m⁻³

Lowest bloom month
mean 0.149 mg m⁻³
+8.7%
CHL change 1993–2025
decadal trend
Monthly Chlorophyll-a · Gulf of Guinea · Jan 1993 – Dec 2025
Full 33-year record of monthly mean surface chlorophyll-a (mg m⁻³). The strong seasonal cycle is the dominant signal — CHL peaks each July–August during the boreal summer upwelling season when SST is coldest, and falls to its minimum in February–March when the sea is warmest. This inverse relationship between SST and CHL is the oceanographic heartbeat of the Gulf of Guinea.
Monthly CHL (mg m⁻³)
Long-term trend
Source: CMEMS OCEANCOLOUR_GLO_BGC_L4_MY_009_104 · L4 gap-free monthly · variable: chl · unit: mg m⁻³
"My mother used to count on sardinella. Every season, the canoes came back heavy. The data reveals something unexpected here — chlorophyll shows a modest upward trend, yet the seasonal pattern is changing: the off-season lows are getting lower, and the peak blooms are becoming more variable. The ocean's rhythm is shifting."
🌿
What is chlorophyll-a? Chlorophyll reflects phytoplankton productivity, which forms the foundation of the marine food chain. More chlorophyll means more phytoplankton — the microscopic plants that feed zooplankton, which in turn feed the small fish that fill the nets of communities like Badagry.
🌡️
Why does warming matter? Changes in chlorophyll may indicate shifts in nutrient availability, upwelling, or warming-driven stress. When phytoplankton productivity changes, fish populations and local fisheries may also change — sometimes dramatically and with little warning.
🎣
What does this mean for fishing communities? For local fishing communities, warmer waters may mean longer travel distances before fish can be found. Ocean warming also contributes to marine heat stress and changes in nutrient mixing — the very processes that drive the upwelling blooms the Gulf of Guinea depends on.
CMEMS · MyOcean Viewer Screenshots CHL · February 2025 vs CHL · August 2025 Comparison · Same region · Different periods
CHL · February 2025
CHL · August 2025
The contrast between the February off-season (minimum bloom, 0.149 mg/m³ mean) and the August upwelling peak (maximum bloom, 0.321 mg/m³ mean) reveals the seasonal heartbeat of the Gulf of Guinea. The boreal summer upwelling cools the surface, drives nutrients upward, and ignites the phytoplankton blooms that feed the entire coastal food web. When this seasonal window shortens, the nets come back lighter.
Source: CMEMS MyOcean Viewer · OCEANCOLOUR_GLO_BGC_L4_MY_009_104 · variable: chl · Feb & Aug 2025
SST vs Chlorophyll — The Inverse Relationship · Annual Means 1993–2025
When the sea is coldest, upwelling brings nutrients to the surface and phytoplankton bloom. When SST rises, stratification increases, upwelling weakens, and chlorophyll falls. This scatter plot of annual means shows the negative correlation between SST and CHL — as the Gulf of Guinea warms, the productive upwelling season shortens and weakens.
1993–2002
2003–2012
2013–2025
Source: Annual means of thetao and chl · both CMEMS products as cited above
Seasonal CHL Cycle — Decade by Decade · Monthly Climatology
Mean chlorophyll-a by calendar month across three decades. The peak bloom amplitude has remained strong, but the winter minimum (Jan–Apr) has declined in the most recent decade — meaning the off-season is getting less productive. The ocean's quiet months are getting quieter.
1993–2002
2003–2012
2013–2025
Source: Monthly climatological means computed from full CHL time series by decade

When the Green Fades,
the Net Comes Back Empty

How energy flows from riverine nutrients to coastal fishing communities — and how much has been lost in thirty years.

Toggle between eras to see the food web contract
Act II · Green Ocean · Gulf of Guinea · Badagry, Nigeria

When the Green Fades,
the Nets Come Back Empty

A marine food chain Sankey tracing the journey from riverine nutrients through phytoplankton and zooplankton to the sardinella nets of Badagry — and the human cost of what is being lost.

OCEANCOLOUR_GLO_BGC_L4_MY_009_104 · GLOBAL_MULTIYEAR_BGC_001_029 · 1993–2025
1993–1997 baseline flow
2020–2024 diminished flow
🌡️
+0.72°C
Warming since 1993 · GoG
As SST rises, the thermocline strengthens. Cooler, nutrient-rich deep water can no longer reach the surface. The upwelling weakens, and with it, the river of life that feeds the coast.
🐟
−59%
Fish catch decline · Ghana · 1993–2019
The canoes still go out before dawn. But the nets come back lighter. Sardinella, the fish that once defined the Gulf of Guinea's coastal economy, are moving deeper, moving offshore, moving away.
🏘️
−50%
Projected catch loss · mid-century
2 million people depend on these fisheries. Under continued warming, half the catch potential could be gone by 2050 — even with well-managed fisheries. The food web has no buffer left.
"My mother used to count on sardinella. Every season, the canoes came back heavy. Then around the mid-2000s, the nets started coming back lighter. The ocean did not empty overnight — it emptied slowly, stage by stage, node by node, exactly as this diagram shows."
— Amara, Badagry, Nigeria · Ocean Sessions Dataviz Challenge 2026
WHEN THE WATER REMEMBERS · Ocean Sessions Dataviz Challenge 2026 · Copernicus Marine Service
Marine food chain flow data based on CMEMS biogeochemical reanalysis patterns for the Gulf of Guinea · Values illustrative of peer-reviewed findings
"The seasonal pulse is still there — but it is changing shape. The cold season blooms, the nets fill. When the cold season shortens, the nets come back lighter."
Act III · Sea Level Anomaly · SEALEVEL_GLO_PHY_L4_MY · DUACS Altimetry
Water In The
Living Room
sla · Monthly mean · Jan 1993 – Sep 2025 · Gulf of Guinea · mm above 1993 baseline
"Every rainy season in Badagry, the flooding starts a little earlier and ends a little later. The data shows why. In 1993, sea level anomaly in the Gulf of Guinea oscillated around zero. By 2024, the entire record had shifted — the ocean floor has not changed, but the water above it sits 106 mm higher than it did when I was born."
CMEMS · MyOcean Viewer Screenshots SLA · April 2004 vs SLA · April 2024 Comparison · Same region · Different periods
SLA · April 2004
SLA · April 2024
Twenty years apart, same month, same region. In April 2004 the sea level anomaly across the Gulf of Guinea was near neutral — the ocean sitting close to its long-term baseline. By April 2024, the anomaly had reached 141.8mm — the highest single month ever recorded in the dataset. The colour shift between these two frames is not a different palette: it is 20 years of rising water made visible.
Source: CMEMS MyOcean Viewer · SEALEVEL_GLO_PHY_L4_MY · DUACS altimetry · April 2004 & April 2024
106mm
Total SLR since 1993
over 32 years
3.32
mm per year rise rate
above global average
141mm
Highest SLA on record
April 2024
514%
SLA increase 1993–2025
decadal mean change
Monthly Sea Level Anomaly · Gulf of Guinea · Jan 1993 – Sep 2025
Raw monthly SLA values (mm) with the long-term linear trend overlay. In the early 1990s, SLA oscillated symmetrically around zero — some months above, some below. By the mid-2010s, negative SLA months had almost disappeared. By 2023–2024, every single month was above 75mm. The entire distribution has shifted upward and is still accelerating.
Monthly SLA (mm)
Linear trend (3.32 mm/yr)
Years above 90mm annual mean
Source: CMEMS SEALEVEL_GLO_PHY_L4_MY · DUACS gridded altimetry · unit: converted from metres to mm · 1 mm = 0.001 m
Annual Mean SLA · Gulf of Guinea · 1993–2025
Annual averages of monthly SLA. In 1993, the annual mean was just 5mm above baseline. By 2024, it had reached 114mm — a 22-fold increase in the annual departure from the long-term mean. The acceleration is visible: the slope of the 2010s and 2020s is steeper than the 1990s and 2000s.
Annual mean SLA (mm)
Linear trend
Source: same as above · annual means of monthly SLA values
Three Variables Together — SST × SLA × CHL · Annual Means · Normalized
All three variables normalized to a 0–1 scale for direct comparison. The story in one panel: SST climbs steadily (warming ocean), SLA rises sharply (the sea climbs the shore), and CHL shows a complex relationship — still responding to seasonal upwelling but increasingly variable as the physical conditions change. Three signals, one story.
SST (normalized)
SLA (normalized)
CHL (normalized)
Source: Annual means of all three CMEMS variables · normalized min-max per variable · 1993–2025
"The sea is not retreating anymore. It has decided to stay. 106 millimetres in 32 years — that is the height of a child's hand above the waterline that used to be safe."
Resolution · What This Means · What Comes Next
The Ocean Has Spoken.
Now the World Must Listen.

Three variables. Thirty-two years. One coherent story. The Gulf of Guinea is warming at +0.22°C per decade — faster than most of the world's oceans. Its sea level has risen 106 millimetres since 1993 and is still accelerating. Its seasonal chlorophyll cycle — the heartbeat that drives the entire coastal food web — is shifting in ways that directly shorten the productive fishing season for communities like Badagry.

These are not projections. They are measurements. Real data, downloaded from the Copernicus Marine Service, processed, and visualized from the perspective of a coastline that has been absorbing these changes for three decades while the rest of the world debated whether they were real.

+0.22°C
Warming per decade
Gulf of Guinea SST
106 mm
Sea level rise
since 1993
>50%
Projected catch loss
by mid-century
200M+
People on West Africa's
coastline at risk

For Amara's family in Badagry — for every fishing community along this coastline, from Abidjan to Douala — the question is no longer whether the ocean is changing. The question is whether the decisions being made in laboratories, policy rooms, and international forums will keep pace with a sea that is not waiting for consensus.

"The ocean has been speaking for thirty years. In Badagry, we have been listening. The data confirms what our grandmothers already knew — and what our children will live with, unless we act." — Amara · Badagry, Nigeria · Gulf of Guinea Coastline
Data & Methodology
Sea Surface Temperature anomalies are computed relative to the 1993–2016 climatological mean (25.39°C) from CMEMS GLOBAL_MULTIYEAR_PHY_001_030 (GLORYS12, cmems_mod_glo_phy_my_0.083deg_P1M-m, variable: thetao). Sea Level Anomaly data from SEALEVEL_GLO_PHY_L4_MY (DUACS gridded altimetry), converted from metres to millimetres. Chlorophyll-a from OCEANCOLOUR_GLO_BGC_L4_MY_009_104 (GlobColour L4, gap-free monthly, mg m⁻³) — L4 interpolated product used specifically because the Gulf of Guinea's persistent ITCZ cloud belt renders L3 daily products unreliable for this region. All data covers the Gulf of Guinea region (approximately 5°W–9°E, 0–6°N). Known limitations: GLORYS12 exhibits regional temperature biases of <0.4°C; SLA values reflect regional dynamic sea level and may differ from global mean sea level estimates.
WHEN THE WATER REMEMBERS · Ocean Sessions Dataviz Challenge 2026 · Copernicus Marine Service
Real data downloaded from CMEMS: GLOBAL_MULTIYEAR_PHY_001_030 · SEALEVEL_GLO_PHY_L4_MY · OCEANCOLOUR_GLO_BGC_L4_MY_009_104 · Gulf of Guinea region · 1993–2025
